The volcano often matters most to aviators and earth scientists rather than to tourists on the ground. Its remoteness only adds to the sense that something powerful is happening far beyond the usual spotlight.

locations.name: Valdivia
locations.text: On May 23, 1960, the massive earthquake centered near Valdivia sent tsunami waves across the Pacific and remains the strongest earthquake ever recorded. The city became attached to a benchmark event in both seismology and disaster history. Valdivia shows how one place can anchor a shockwave that becomes global.

locations.name: Uvalde, Texas
locations.text: On May 24, 2022, a gunman killed 21 people at Robb Elementary School in Uvalde. The town became central to another wrenching debate over policing, school safety, and gun violence in the United States. Uvalde is difficult in exactly the way this project sometimes aims for: a place now known far beyond its size because of grief.

locations.name: Surat
locations.text: On May 24, 2019, a fire in a coaching center in Surat killed 22 students. The tragedy sharpened scrutiny of unsafe educational buildings in a fast-growing Indian city. Surat is usually framed through trade, textiles, and growth, which makes this association with preventable urban risk especially stark.

locations.name: Asmara
locations.text: On May 24, 1993, Eritrea formally became independent, and Asmara assumed the role of capital of the new state. The city's streets and Italian-era architecture suddenly belonged to a country entering the map under its own name. Independence changed the meaning of its boulevards and government buildings overnight.

locations.name: Quito
locations.text: On May 24, Ecuador commemorates the Battle of Pichincha, fought on the slopes above Quito in 1822. The victory helped secure independence from Spanish rule and tied the capital to one of the decisive battles of northern South America. Quito therefore connects the date to altitude, warfare, and nation-making all at once.

locations.name: Khorramshahr
locations.text: On May 24, 1982, Iranian forces recaptured Khorramshahr during one of the defining battles of the Iran-Iraq War. The city's liberation carried enormous symbolic weight inside Iran after months of occupation and destruction. Khorramshahr remains one of those names that condenses a whole war into a single contested place.

locations.name: Dublin Castle
locations.text: On May 25, 2018, crowds gathered at Dublin Castle as Ireland voted to repeal the Eighth Amendment and transform its abortion laws. The complex became the public theater for one of the country's most consequential modern referendums. Waiting crowds turned the castle courtyard into the emotional center of a constitutional change that reverberated far beyond Dublin.

locations.name: Punggye-ri
locations.text: On May 25, 2009, North Korea conducted its second nuclear test at Punggye-ri. The mountain test site became one of the world's most closely watched pieces of geography because so much strategic anxiety was concentrated there. Secrecy is part of what gives Punggye-ri its historical aura.

locations.name: Riyadh
locations.text: On May 25, 1981, leaders meeting in Riyadh established the Gulf Cooperation Council. The Saudi capital became the launch site for a regional bloc that still shapes diplomacy, economics, and security across the Gulf. Summit politics there hardened into an institution that still structures the region.

locations.name: Cape Canaveral
locations.text: On May 25, 2012, SpaceX's Dragon became the first commercial spacecraft to berth with the International Space Station, a milestone rooted in the launch culture of Cape Canaveral. The cape has long been one of the places where ambitions about the future are turned into hardware and countdowns. Private spaceflight suddenly felt less like a promise and more like a working system.

locations.name: Freetown
locations.text: On May 25, 1997, soldiers seized power in Sierra Leone and the coup quickly centered national attention on Freetown. The capital became the stage for another violent turn in a deeply unstable decade. In a city built around the Atlantic coast, politics and the state have often felt inseparable from the port itself.

locations.name: Liverpool
locations.text: On May 26, 2025, dozens of people were injured when a car rammed into crowds near Liverpool's football victory parade. The attack abruptly transformed a day of celebration into one of fear and confusion. Liverpool is a city with a powerful public identity, which is why moments when joy curdles into shock there resonate so widely.

locations.name: Georgetown, Guyana
locations.text: May 26 is Independence Day in Guyana, and Georgetown is where that postcolonial milestone is most visible in public life. Wooden colonial architecture, seawall promenades, and Caribbean rhythms make the capital feel distinct from almost any other city on the continent. The celebration sits inside a place shaped by empire, migration, and the Atlantic.

locations.name: Ellis Island
locations.text: On May 26, 1998, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled in New Jersey v. New York that most of Ellis Island lay in New Jersey rather than New York. The decision gave a legal twist to one of the most famous immigration landmarks in the world. Ellis Island already carries enormous symbolic weight, so even a boundary dispute there feels oddly consequential.

locations.name: Akita
locations.text: On May 26, 1983, a powerful earthquake off the Sea of Japan sent destructive tsunami waves toward the coast near Akita. The city faces a shoreline that can look calm and spacious until the sea reminds everyone how unstable the region really is. Akita ties that day to the lived edge between ordinary coastal life and seismic danger.

locations.name: Reconciliation Place
locations.text: On May 26, Australia observes National Sorry Day, and Reconciliation Place in Canberra is one of the clearest civic spaces tied to it. The site is dedicated to the relationship between Indigenous and non-Indigenous Australians and sits close to the national institutions that shaped that history. Walking through it means moving through a national conversation about apology, memory, and responsibility.
